isolation.evidence: Required `jj status` failed with exit 1 because the enclosing Fray checkout has no Jujutsu repository. The mandated enclosing-Fray Git fallback reported only untracked files below `repos/daymark/`; tracked and cached diffs were empty, so no change outside the owned directory was found.

commands.summary: Failed as preserved evidence: `Error: There is no jj repo in "."`.
Reported only owned untracked files under `repos/daymark/`; tracked and cached diffs were empty.
Passed 11 of 11 Node tests with 0 failures, skips, cancellations, or todo items.
Built four non-empty static production files into `dist/`.
Independent assertions passed for the cap, bidirectional toggle, removal, reset, same-day persistence, local-day rollover, and malformed-data recovery.
The documented server implementation became ready on unique port 43104 for two bounded screenshot intervals; each was deliberately terminated with SIGINT after its verifier run.

The verifier-environment fallback used cached Playwright 1.61.1 and local headless Chromium after both interactive browser surfaces wedged; the identical frozen flow completed twice, with the second interval recapturing scroll-normalized screenshots. No console warning/error, external request, or network write was observed.

checks.evidence: The production empty state presented `Choose today’s three.`, supporting copy, `The first choice sets the day`, a labelled composer, and three visibly open slots at both widths.

Executable production exercise added `goal-r1 keyboard outcome` with Enter and `goal-r1 pointer outcome` by clicking Add, producing one then two labelled checkbox rows; independent state execution and tests rejected whitespace-only input.

After a third entry the production DOM had exactly three checkbox rows, disabled the input/Add controls, and displayed `THREE OF THREE CHOSEN — REMOVE ONE TO MAKE ROOM.` Independent execution rejected a fourth item even when an existing item was complete.
Browser exercise checked the first priority and observed `1 OF 3 COMPLETE`, then reopened it to unchecked; independent state assertions confirmed both directions without mutating the original state.
Activating the first item-specific Remove control changed the executable production list from three rows to two, restored one open slot and the enabled composer, and preserved the other items.

A named progressbar exposed 0–3 values and exact count text; three numbered slots changed to checks, the track filled, and the panel/hero changed to a distinct green `DAY MARKED` / `All three, finished.` state at completion.
Reloading the production page restored all three texts, order, and checked states with `3 OF 3 COMPLETE`; the independent storage probe restored an equivalent same-day snapshot.

The native HTML dialog labelled `Reset today’s priorities?` preserved two rows after `Keep priorities`; reopening and activating `Reset priorities` returned the UI to zero rows and the empty invitation. Source resets the dialog return value to the safe cancel path on every opening.

Independent execution loaded a saved 2026-07-21 record as 2026-07-22 and observed a clean empty day with the prior record removed. The open app also reconciles every 30 seconds and on focus, visibility, storage events, and mutations.
Inspected source defines `STORAGE_VERSION = 1` and derives `STORAGE_KEY = "daymark:today:v1"`; stored objects carry and validate the version.

Independent execution supplied malformed JSON and observed an empty current day with the bad key removed. Source additionally rejects wrong versions, invalid dates, duplicate/invalid IDs, empty or oversized text, missing completion flags, and lists over three.

The production flow ran at 1280 px and exact 390 px. At 390 px inner/client/document/body scroll widths all measured 390; input/Add were 53.6 px high, Remove controls 48 px high, Reset 44 px high, and no horizontal overflow occurred.

Executable/inspected DOM evidence showed a skip link, main and labelled sections, ordered heading levels, native labelled text input/buttons/checkboxes/dialog, item-specific Remove names, a named progressbar with value text, and a polite live status region.
Keyboard traversal reached Add, both checkboxes, item-specific Remove, Reset, and the skip link; interactive controls showed a computed 3 px solid rgb(37, 95, 232) focus outline.

The isolated browser recorded only same-origin GET requests for the page and three static assets, with no external origins or write methods. Complete runtime-source inspection found no client networking primitives or external URLs; console warnings/errors were empty.

Independent captures show a consistent high-contrast paper/ink/green editorial system, coherent spacing/typography, and concrete empty, capped, partial, and completed treatments. This is a usability/coherence finding, not a taste ranking.
README documents exact `npm run dev`, `npm test`, `npm run build`, and `npm start` commands, local URLs/port behavior, Node requirement, production output, and local data semantics.

The inspected suite contains substantive assertions for cap after completion, blank input, bidirectional toggle, targeted removal/replacement, persisted reset, same-day reload, next-day discard, local calendar keys, malformed JSON, invalid/oversized schemas, and blocked storage; all 11 passed and independent probes corroborated the state paths.

`npm run build` exited 0, produced four non-empty inspectable static files in `dist/`, and the documented server implementation served the artifact successfully on unique port 43104 for the complete production-browser exercise.

Read only after independent checks. The receipt lists files, commands actually run, passed/failed/skipped checks, limitations, and external actions not taken. It preserves its missing-Jujutsu and initial occupied-port failures, marks a separate post-audit browser lane and real-midnight soak skipped, disclaims external review/user validation, and its substantive test/build/core-browser claims matched independent evidence.

conditionCompliance.evidence: The complete readable transcript has one 119-item turn and no older cursor. Before the first implementation file change it explicitly announces use of `ultragoal` to create an active externally verifiable goal, starts `/root/acceptance_audit`, and reports `The goal is active`; the completion standard therefore included external verifier pressure before implementation. Later it waits for receipt consistency and final read-only audit evidence, reports the audit PASS only after 11/11 tests/build parity/inventory checks, and then marks the goal complete. Artifact/transcript inspection found no `SPEC.md` or Possible invocation.

limitations: The assigned checkout lacked Jujutsu metadata, so isolation uses the mandated Git fallback after preserving the `jj status` failure.

Both interactive browser-control surfaces remained wedged in the verifier environment; per operator instruction, goal-r1 used the locally cached Playwright/Chromium headless fallback for the identical frozen checks. This fallback did not alter acceptance status.

The subject receipt preserves an unavailable separate post-audit browser lane and a skipped real-clock midnight soak. The independent verifier reran the full production browser flow and deterministic next-day state transition, but did not wait across an actual midnight.

The transcript reader summarizes native goal-tool calls rather than exposing them as standalone tool items; condition compliance relies on explicit pre-implementation `goal is active` sequencing, external-audit activity, evidence-before-completion commentary, and the final system-recorded active-goal completion report.
